title: vimeo_1202349380
displayTitle: 2026-06-18 - Transport and Infrastructure Committee - 2026/27 Estimates for Vote Building and Construction
topic: 2026/27 Estimates for Vote Building and Construction
description: Thursday, 18 June 2026 | Transport and Infrastructure Committee 2026/27 Estimates for Vote Building and Construction Hearing of Evidence: Minister for Building and Construction Hon Chris Penk James Hartley, (Acting) Deputy Secretary – Building, Resources and Markets Stephanie Greathead, General Manager – Building and Tenancy David Hall, General Manager – Building System Performance Antonia Reid, Policy Director – Building System Performance Dave Gittings, Manager – Building Performance and Engineering Matthew McDermott, Manager – Building Performance and Resilience Suzannah Toulmin, Manager – Consenting and Practitioners Policy Simon Thomas, Head of Building System Delivery and Assurance Micheal Warren, Manager – Systems and Markets Policy Fiona Hill, Team Leader – System and Markets Policy Ministry of Business, Innovation and Employment—Hīkina Whakatutuki David Hall, General Manager – Building System Performance Stephanie Greathead, General Manager – Building and Tenancy Simon Thomas, Head of Building System Delivery and Assurance Micheal Warren, Policy Director (Acting) – Building System Performance Dave Gittings, Manager – Building Performance and Engineering, Building System Performance Matthew McDermott, Manager – Building Performance and Resilience Policy, Building System Performance Suzannah Toulmin, Manager – Consenting and Practitioners Policy, Building System Performance Fiona Hill, Team Leader – System and Markets Policy, Building System Performance
descriptionSummary: 2026/27 Estimates for Vote Building and Construction. Minister for Building and Construction Hon Chris Penk. James Hartley, (Acting) Deputy Secretary – Building, Resources and Markets

chunks.text: Great. Do

you want anybody else to come and join you at the table, or are you- I haven't been able to persuade any more than one so far. But with your permission, Mr. Chair. You're very shy in retiring all of you. I meant to invite you in earlier, and... As time goes on, depending on your questions- They can come. Yeah... we might call upon- Sure... particular subject matter experts.

Sure. But welcome. Welcome, all of you. Good to see some familiar faces back again who've worked very hard on many bits of legislation. Yes, sir. And we'll use your process. We'll open up for a few minutes of anything you wanted to touch on, and then we'll go to questions. Wonderful. Well, good afternoon, Mr. Chair and team. Thank you very much for having me. I'm really looking forward to the opportunity to discuss matters that are dear to all of our hearts, I'm sure, building and construction.

I think I'll make my opening remarks pretty brief, because obviously we don't have that much time together, and I'm just really keen to place myself in the hands of the committee in terms of areas of interest for you. I think I'd be remiss, though, if I didn't make three acknowledgments. The first is that we've got a building sector in this country that has many men and women who are hardworking, talented, determined. They've faced a difficult time in the last few years, for many of them, and different by different regions of the country and

according to their place in the process and so on. But it has been a tough last several years. But my hope is that we can reform our systems in a way that will make it easier and faster and more affordable for them, of course, and for those who benefit from the built environment in this country.

My second acknowledgment is of the committee itself. You've worked very hard, if I may say, Mr. Chair, you and your team, to provide great advice and suggested changes, all of which I think for many have been accepted in terms of legislation, and goodness knows there's been a bit of that, and there may yet be more too, through some combination of stages in the remaining parliamentary term.

So thank you for that. Genuinely grateful. And I always make that point when I have the opportunity to give a second reading speech in the house. But allow me to- We always appreciate it... thank you. And the other one is the MB officials who have been working on various items of reform, I think I can say have been consistently hardworking, dedicated.

And the feedback I get from the sector about the level of interaction and engagement from the MB officials has been highly impressive. So I do want to take the opportunity to put it on record that I'm grateful for them. And on that note, of course, you have a continuation, effectively, of this session at the point that I depart, and you'll have a chance to talk with them. So I don't want to steal their thunder in terms of anything they'll say. And obviously, if there are organizational matters, I'll obviously answer as best I can for the committee, but just to acknowledge the fact they'll be

available to you as well for the technically second part of the hearing. But other than that, through you, Mr. Chair, very happy to engage and of course answer any questions you may have. Thank you. And thank you for your generous comments, and always thank you for your generous comments during various debates when you... So we always appreciate that. Genuinely grateful, but thank you. Thank you. Okay. I did offer the first question, and probably the second question at least to Arena.

Thank you. Arena, away you go. Mr. Chair. Yeah, you're welcome. One and two there first. No. Thank you, Minister. You've got a busy program of reforms, and many of them we agree with. Yep. And some we push you to go further and faster. And we're coming from different perspectives. I would have prioritized workforce constraints. Your government constantly refers to planning system constraints as the major ones. But I want to ask you genuinely About three things-... that are something we find common ground on, but disagreement around the

edges in terms of whether they are improving constraints on supply. The first is overseas product. Yeah. Last year you were saying that about 250,000 products will be approved by the end of this year. Yeah. It currently looks like about 19,000. This is a market that's long been characterized by duopoly, that's vertically integrated, has exclusive distribution arrangements in the New Zealand market.

Is this a regulatory problem at all? Is this a market structure problem? Thank you for that. Were you intending to simply ask me- Yeah, I've got a couple ones... the one question? Yeah. Okay. The second one is granny flats. Yeah. Building small dwellings cheaply, we support that. I think everyone around this committee wants that to work. Thank you. But the pipes aren't in the ground.

And MBIE projected out that between 9,000 and 12,000 were going to be built. Yesterday, the housing minister couldn't tell me how many have been built. Yeah. And the third question is about infrastructure building. Probably the thing where we have the most agreement around the house, that this needs to be bipartisan and long-term.

But New Zealand's market under your watch is concentrating. A smaller number of firms are capable of undertaking large scale civil and construction work, particularly water infrastructure. Specialist engineering is being lost in New Zealand. Homegrown company building capacity is scarce now, not even just looking at the future pipeline.

So it's raising prices at the top end of town, but will rise prices later. That's going to create real issues for building and construction in coming years. Great. Okay, thank you. Mr. Joell, answer those or respond to those three questions in the same order that they were posed. In terms of overseas products, I'm grateful for the support around the house for the proposition that we need to have a more competitive supply of building products.

Of course, overseas approvals doesn't mean that we want a greater proportion even than the existing 90% of products that are from overseas to be introduced. And certainly we don't want to make life more difficult for local suppliers and manufacturers and no one would suggest that's the case. But I feel the need to place that on the record, for the sake of completeness. In terms of the number that have been approved, it's always been the case that with a relatively new regime that would need to proceed carefully, we wanted

to take a conservative approach. So while of course the overall impact of the regime is to open up competition by making it easier for products to be approved in this country, nevertheless, we don't want to throw the baby out the bathwater and of course risk any kind of lowering of quality, sustainability or safety of materials and therefore obviously the built environment.

In terms of the numbers, it might be that our MBIE friends are able to advise more particularly what the running total is. But I know that there are a couple of big ticket items in terms of certification systems overseas whereby credible and comparable jurisdictions have whole swathes of products, similar products in some cases, that are able to be approved.

So I'm not bothered by the fact that we will proceed at the pace that we can safely do so, because obviously we don't want to risk any suggestion whatsoever that we would have lower confidence as a result of materials being approved in this country that wouldn't have been approved if they had jumped through all the hoops that we're trying to avoid.

And my final comment in that regard, if I may, is simply that improving competition should place downward pressure on price, and over time that will increasingly be so as the shipments come in. Goodness knows, supply shocks and other external factors associated with those will, of course, play a part.

But it's not just downward pressure on prices we're after, it's greater innovation, including for the purpose of improved sustainability of products such as we see in overseas jurisdictions that we can and should benefit from in this country. And of course, simply to allow more options and choices for homeowners and those who would design and build their homes and commercial environment and so on.

Can I just pause for breath in case there's a particular statistical input from our friends at MBIE in terms of the number of products approved so far? Yep. Did you want to- So I did a quick calculation. Did you want to introduce yourself? Oh, apologies for butting in. Yeah. Sorry. David Paul, I'm the General Manager of the Building System Performance branch at MBIE.

Thank you, as the minister said, for the opportunity to talk and for all the work you've done most recently on the earthquake building stuff. It's a big program of work that we've brought through to you. So look, just to answer the question, and I guess first thing is a little bit of a proviso. The way the scheme works, as you're probably very familiar with, is that in some cases we approve standards, and then there's a whole lot of products that are approved under that standard that would then get the automatic entry into the New Zealand market.

For those standards, we have no idea how many products may be sitting behind them and relying on that standard. What we do know for the ones that are more product specific is that there are slightly north of 130,000 products that have been approved so far, and there'll be a lot more under the various standards.

Now, those are products that have been approved. They're not necessarily in the New Zealand market, but they are products that have been approved through the scheme. Thank you. That's really helpful in my view, to have that number, which I wasn't fast enough to find myself. Going to your second point, Ms. Williams, if I may, in terms of granny flats, the estimate, and I do emphasize an estimate, was that some 13,000 such new- accommodation types whose statues were dwelling, although they are a dwelling, a minor dwelling

of sorts. Extra 13,000 over 10 years. Obviously, we're very early in the process from the point of view that it was only in mid-January of this year that the rules came into effect, both in terms of the resource management side of things, which is led by Chris Bishop rather than me, but also on the building consent side of things.

So, in the intervening five or so months, I know there have been a number of such granny flats constructed, and indeed I was in northwest Auckland yesterday, my own electorate actually, I'm happy to report, and attended the official opening of one such granny flat. Now, there's one here, there's one there.

Clearly the pipeline will build, but the word is getting out there. And so obviously I can't wield a crystal ball to tell the member exactly what the numbers will be over 10 years, and it may be fewer, it may be more than 13,000. But I think the principle of the thing is important, which is that we are proving that it is possible to use existing infrastructure, where there is already a residential zone and usage of a property.

And of course, it's a detached dwelling of a single story up to 70 square meters on an existing parcel of land where there is already an existing dwelling. So from that point of view, we think it's helpful between that and the self-certified elements whereby these relatively low-risk structures are signed off in effect by the licensed building practitioners and other qualified tradies who are doing the work.

So we think it's helpful both in terms of system reform more generally, but also the particular outcomes for those who might or might not be grannies, per the retail description of granny flats, but they might also be younger New Zealanders, they might be farm workers, there might be others, students, au pairs, other boarders who can benefit.

And so I pass to the third and final question or comment regarding infrastructure. Technically, to the extent that civil construction and other engineering falls outside the Building Act, that's technically not within my remit. But I can say clearly there are market forces that we need to be alive to. We don't want to have a market in which there isn't competition either for practical reasons or, heaven forbid, ways that the regulatory regime would force a concentration that you've described.

So we know that's something we need to be mindful of and certainly talking with ministerial colleagues who are variously the Minister for Infrastructure and the Minister for Commerce and Consumer Affairs, that's something we need to be mindful of, for sure. If I can ask a different primary question then. Yep. Just the budget contains no new spending, and reasonably significant cuts alongside a pretty ambitious reform program.

So I'm trying to understand what are the trade-offs, what are the things that lose out because of that? It's a 6.1% decrease in the vote whilst at a time where, say, safety standards are a real focus. Asbestos contaminated fire doors this year, increasing lead taps, blind cords that pose a serious risk to children, failing imported window systems that make walls leak.

Those things indicate that we have ongoing issues in New Zealand's buildings around maintaining quality and safety. I'm not expecting you to solve it all, but is it getting worse? Good question. I think there's quite a lot in that question. To speak to blind cords, technically it's a matter of safety as opposed to building construction.

So a ministerial colleague will be able to answer questions you have in that space. I won't speak on behalf of Brooke van Velden who would be the relevant minister, I think. But in any case, it's not me. In terms of-- apologies, that might be standards, that might be Cameron Brewer.

But in any case, I can't help you specifically on that one, but of course, it's an important issue. In terms of leaking of windows or doors, these are not new issues or problems. But the thing I'm really pleased about the approach that MBIE has taken is that they are very mindful of the need to enforce strongly standards that were already in place and remain in place, and our broadening of overseas approvals pathways doesn't actually change the fact that when we have a set of rules, they must be enforced, and they can't be

undermined by fraud and forgery. There have been recent instances that have been determined, and I'm very pleased to say that MBIE has initiated enforcement action against those entities behaving in that way. Because of course that's not good for the consumer, but it's also an exercise in creating a non-level playing field for those New Zealand manufacturers in particular who work hard and play by the rules and deserve not to be disadvantaged in relative terms.

So, I think clearly there will always be issues against which we need to be vigilant in the built environment performance systems. But I don't think anything in terms of the current budget would undermine that. Indeed, we are working hard to ensure that there is enforcement, including of the BIPA, the Building Products Information- Requirements... Requirements. Thank you.

It was 50/50 between that and regulation or regime. Anyway, requirements it is. And where there have been reductions in budget, that really reflects the discontinuation of programs that are no longer needed. For example, for the passage of time since where the tight homes processes were always due to play out and finish at that time.

That, I guess, is my question. Which parts of the vote are the things that are not a priority for you, where MB could be finding cuts? Because with time with officials later, we'll be able to delve into what their plan is to make those pretty ambitious cuts that are bigger than Treasury has ever let any previous government book. But in terms of, is it the building consent system?

Probably not. Is it the occupational licensing and regulation system? Well, no. We're seeing pretty hefty changes there. Is it product assurance? You've said it's not. Is it building code? There are no changes coming, but you've already got a big program of work on some changes there, including to insulation and windows. Is it standards work? Is it self-cert? Is it earthquake-prone building?

Yeah. I think I've identified one. The other one is in terms of standards work, the ongoing work needed to ensure that our building standards, as part of a general standards regime, is fit for purpose and aligned with Australia particularly. That will continue. What we did see in the most recent couple of years is that a large effort has been made to bring back together, to re-joint New Zealand standards with Australian standards, because over a period of time they drifted apart and that makes no sense for us relative to our trans-Tasman neighbor.

And with that work, having had a particular injection of cash over a two-year period, and that having come to an end with us having played catch up, effectively, the ongoing work in a business as usual kind of way will continue now at a more modest level to ensure that the building team plays its part to make sure that our standards are fit for purpose, appropriate, modern, and aligned with Australia.

Just a- Last one, yeah... very brief question about that then. I'm worried about the capacity of this team. This is a great team. You constantly thank them for their work in your speeches. We have been able to get some assurance to get on board with your program of reform because it is work that is conducted to a very high standard. You want to do insurance, you want to do joint and several liability.

Those are two things that flummoxed the Law Commission and MB in a previous iteration. How are we going to do this stuff? Are you going to your budget minister's meetings and advocating for this department? Why are we seeing some of the biggest cuts to this team being made in baseline savings, which will result in a 6% cut that's bigger than Treasury ever lets a labor government make?

Yeah. I don't understand the final characterization about- A 6% cut- Yes... sounds unachievable. Well- Is your chief executive out there saying that this is achievable? Did you say that Treasury hasn't allowed previous governments to make cuts? Because it's impossible to look at a department that's doing this much work and say, "Where are you going to find the cuts?"

Well, I would say, as you've rightly acknowledged, they are doing the work. They're doing it extremely well, and they've been recognized in official terms by way of awards internal to MB as well as the recognition I've quite rightly given them. They're getting on and doing the work. They are achieving things that previous iterations of MB could only have dreamed about, including in terms of joint civil liability.

And you'll literally lose one in 20 of their staff under this current model. As long as they continue to provide outstanding outcomes, then I will be very happy. If that were not the case, then we would need to consider a range of factors, of which resourcing would be one, but only one. And in the meantime, I don't think you can argue with the product, and to me, that's more important than a line item and a dollar sign, and an FTE headcount. They're being smart about the way they're doing it, and I, for one, am really grateful for that.

Okay. I've got a couple of supps. Dan. Thanks, Mr. Chair. Thanks, Minister. Just going back to the building products, that's fantastic news that 130,000 overseas products have been approved. Any idea of just, I guess, how do you get the information out there to the sector so that they actually start taking up those opportunities? So what is the plan for going to disseminate that as quickly as possible so that we can start, we as consumers, and can start seeing the benefits of those 150,000 products?

Yeah. If I may say, I think that's a really astute question that Mr. Bigweir poses because I've had to give a lot of thought to the fact that we have on paper now a much more enabling system. And yes, we've been careful to ensure there are safeguards to make sure that we don't lose the quality, safety, sustainability that we need.

But I think the main challenge is an understanding and a willingness of those who specify the products and building consent applications in the first instance. Second, where there are changes that can be made to provide a different or better or more readily available product, that's traditionally been quite tough.

And some of that is come... I'm sometimes told by those in the industry that there are anti-competitive practices by certain providers. I'm not a one-person commerce commission. I can't determine that to be the case or not. But I can say that where every opportunity is available to speak to architects and design LVPs, to talk to building consent authorities, and also to amend the regulations that we have now, whereby you can substitute in products more easily that weren't specified. For example, plasterboard type A

may be less available due to supply shop reasons, or turns out to be less affordable than to substitute plasterboard type B or C, should be much easier than it has in the past. We've made that change. Whether or not that's broadly known and acted on is probably an open question, which is why I say your query is a good one.

So that's ongoing work, I think, for all of us. Great. Thank you. Thank you. Can I ask a supp on that? You got a supp on that one as well. It's time. Do you have a supp? It's around the standards. Is that on the same issue? Yeah, sort of. Okay. Away you go. Okay, and then Dorina. Minister, I just want to ask you about MBIE's stewardship of building standards. I've had some really concerning conversations with stakeholders recently.

They're concerned that MBIE's ability to update standards in response to new information, knowledge, changes in design of materials, is just not responsive enough. They gave me an example and maybe something you want to talk to officials about later. I'm sure you can ask them after your session here. They were told that to change a couple of words in a standard relating to concrete could cost up to $50,000 in MBIE time, that they would have to pay for that.

They've also described their concern that it took six years following the Christchurch earthquakes to update some standards relating to the way concrete performs. Now, I know that's potentially before your time in this office here as minister. But I'm wondering, do you have confidence in MBIE's stewardship of standards and their ability to respond to the market?

Can I say, I have confidence in the building systems and performance team. They are the ones who I've interacted with on all these matters, including that they have let me know the work that they've done with the broader team, which sits outside building standards and performance in relation to standards. So, I think in ministerial terms, and I'm not trying to reflect, because obviously you raise an important issue.

I think that sits with Commerce and Consumer Affairs. But the visibility that I've had from the building side has been really positive in the time that I've been in the role. I can't obviously speak to past performance. I would say, of course, it's generally the case that the sectors themselves tend to fund changes.

And there are advantages and disadvantages in that. And of course, resources could be made more available, and that would be a legitimate point to be raised. And in fact, I think, Honora Williams might have come close to making such a point, and that would be a choice we could make, and that would speed up things and place less of a burden on the sector to fund any such changes.

Well, I think they appreciate they have a responsibility to develop the technical knowledge and the technical support for a proposed change. I think one of the concerns is that MBIE is not necessarily responsive enough, and that MBIE's own costs for carrying out their evaluations appear to be excessive.

So have you heard similar reports? That's not something of which I've had visibility. I know generally speaking, there is always a tension between the idea that the government, as opposed to the sector, would fund standards developments. But more particularly than that, I can't be helpful.

It might be that that's something that MBIE would be able to comment on, whether now, and I'll leave of you and the Chair as to whether you invite that now or in the follow-up session. I think if it's an issue that officials can deal with, let's do that with officials. Yeah. Otherwise, it'll take too much of your time. Yeah. Okay. Happy. Thank you, Mr. Chair.

Right. Okay. Supplementaries on this. You've done right. Okay, so, I just had one, which is just in terms of the granny flats, it might be one you can deal with very, very quickly. I understand there were some issues with some sorts of paneling which were intended to be caught but weren't. In other words, that they... Yeah, and that's been fixed. Is that correct?

Yeah, I think- I see some nodding going on in the background. That's helpful. Thank you. Yeah. That's my answer then. To which I would simply add, we instituted the granny flats policy with the hope that it would achieve certain aims, which we've discussed and with which you're well familiar. It's been the case since then that we've been careful to ensure we listen to the sector to make sure that if there are other opportunities to broaden the scheme or the effect of the scheme without undermining its purpose, then we would.

And in the case of structural insulated panels or SIPs, these are a building system that already have code mark approval in some cases, and so it would seem anomalous not to be able to include those in granny flat construction. But because of the way that we framed the reasonably basic, straightforward standard nature of timber buildings, they weren't included in our original conception, which is why we've- It's a bit of an oversight, but we will get there.

I think it's a very fair summary, yeah. Yeah. Okay, cool. Right. Carlos. Primary. And did I have another primary floating around somewhere off the state? Okay. Okay. Carlos, where are you? Thank you, Minister, for coming. We heard a lot of feedback from the sector. One of the major issues is the workforce issues. Yeah. So how can budget 2026 actually support the role of the workforce in this sector?

Yeah, thanks for that. I'm grateful for the question because of course, of all the different elements that are needed to produce a great built environment for Kiwis to live, work and play, and learn and do other things. We need not only products, as we've been discussing, and processes, going to the consenting regime. But of course, most importantly, people.

I think workforce are supported, first of all, when we have a good set of rules that enable them to get on and do their work in as quick, easy, and affordable fashion as possible. We want to reduce the stress often on small business owners who are tradies. Not always, but often. And so in that sense, I think the moves that we're making and the resource that we continue to apply to those who are helping to formulate the rules is positive.

I know in the budget elsewhere, and that is to say beyond the building and construction elements, there are helpful moves to provide infrastructure. Horizontal infrastructure in a way that is not relevant to the work that comes under the Building Act, but of course, enables the provision of housing developments and so on, which in turn is useful to the work that people need to do.

And of course, the work that people need to do is important because, it's roughly speaking, one in 10 Kiwis are employed directly or indirectly in property. And of course, we understand the value of the sector To economic growth, including in the regions, as well as all of those who need to have residential, commercial, and infrastructure provided.

So for those reasons, I think there are good stories to be told in the workforce picture, even as I acknowledge, of course, as I did previously, that there have been difficult times for those in the sector, and there are lots of macroeconomic factors that we can analyze and discuss, and even argue over, as far as that goes. But I think overall, I would say the outlook is positive.

The consenting numbers to the year ended April were 16% higher than the previous 12 month period. So, while we don't want to see a boom-bust cycle head towards a boom, we do want to see a steady upward incline of growth, and so far, cautiously optimistic that's what we're seeing this year. Yes. I also want to follow up with one of the pilot program called Earn As You Learn. So is Budget 2026 actually allocated more money to this pilot program to expand to more region?

Yeah. That's right. I think, and it's helpful you've asked that question because I think there is a number of different things we can do to make sure we've got an educated workforce. We do need an educated workforce in the trades because increasingly, we have technology available that is sophisticated. Has the great advantage of enabling huge productivity advances if we are able to use it well, and from a regulatory point of view, we enable that in a sensible fashion. But we're going to need educated Kiwis in the workforce

to do that. Part of the education, of course, starts at the compulsory education sector. But beyond that, and for those who do choose to move on quickly from school environment, they might want to enter into a program such as Earn As You Learn. Which is a little bit like an apprenticeship as a previous generation would have known, but sometimes across different business providers.

So, I think that's a helpful move. And also, of course, going back to the compulsory education sector, to have building and construction qualifications towards what one can walk away from school with, as a young man or woman wanting to make a career in the building and construction trades.

That's a really helpful innovation, to my mind. It's the work of Erica Stanford, not Chris Penk. And while I'm at it, the work of Penny Simmonds in terms of setting up industry skills boards with relevant industry-connected expertise. Again, it's not my work, but I think it's part of that overall picture that's going to be helpful for our workforce issues into the future.

Thank you. Cool. Thanks. Dave. Thanks, Mr. Chair. Just heading to the consenting systems because that seems to has been a big bugbear for a lot of people out there. You've taken some stuff away from the building consent system, so you don't need to go through it. But just want to get a sense on your reform program to speed up building consents, and then secondly, how you're monitoring those changes. So are we actually seeing a reduction in building consent authority processing times?

Any observations that you might have would be helpful. Thanks. Perhaps if you could sort of lay out the things that you'll be thinking of if you get a free run at it over the next 12 months, what are the kind of things that you'd like to see changed in terms of legislative program? Yeah. Thanks very much, Mr. Chair, and to the member for his question. I think there are lots of things we need to do in terms of building consents processing. We want it to be faster.

We want it to be no less rigorous. We want the quality, but then the speed and the affordability also improve, and I know that sounds like an exercise in trying to have it all ways. But I think the lack of productivity we've seen in the sector, by some measures being as low as 1984, pre-internet, let alone AI, and with New Zealand having a much less voluminous built environment, seems to me we can do much better.

I think the answer is to ensure that we have a balanced, proportionate risk-based system. And so the reforms that I'm about to describe, as you've invited, are all geared around thinking about where there is work that is bespoke, or otherwise risky, or those doing the work are less qualified or experienced or able to stand behind their own work.

Where those things are true, we want no less scrutiny than we've had, and perhaps arguably more. But for those relatively low risk types of building, if you think granny flats as being single story structures on existing parcels of land. If you think about qualified registered professionals with consumer protections in place, such as guarantees, warranties, insurance, then we should be more trusting of those.

And so we sort of rebalance the system and make it more nuanced in that sense. So, on that note, self-certification. It's a phrase that has meaning across a number of different fields. One is in terms of granny flats, which we've spoken about already. Another is in terms of plumbers and drain layers, who will be able to certify that their work meets the code in exactly the same way as electricians and gas fitters are already able to do. And I know this committee's well aware of that work, has contributed strongly to it, and in fact, made some very

significant suggestions for improvement at the time that that bill was in front of them. So again, I just want to take the opportunity to thank you for that. And then the other category of self-certification that we've talked about but not yet introduced by way of legislation is the concept that single repeatable designs of relatively straightforward residential dwellings by entities that are able to prove that they have quality assurance systems, have a strong track record of work, can stand behind their own work in the sense of liability if something were to

go wrong, and building at a sufficient scale to make it worth their while- Could be able to self-certify their work into the future. So that's the broad category of self-certification. And then the other ways in which we're looking to make the building consent process faster and easier is to say that building consent authorities, which are mostly councils, some 67, I think, city and district councils at the moment should be able to consolidate their work within a region if they should choose.

So it's voluntary consolidation. It doesn't cut across any other plans or restructuring work in terms of water or amalgamation with a capital A in that general sense. And so to allow them to do that will mean that they can share resources, which they welcome, human resources in terms of the people doing the inspections, doing the consenting, doing the signing off at the end.

And if you're a builder or other tradie in an area and you're building across different lines on a map that represent the jurisdictions of relatively small councils, in some cases, you want the certainty and consistency that you're not getting 67 different interpretations of a single building code according to which side of a line on a map you might be doing the building. So we think that'll be a positive step as well.

And then just on how are we actually going out there in the building consent authorities- Oh, yeah... are you noticing a reduction overall, some councils or building consent authorities doing better than others? Yeah, thank you for that. Yeah, and I apologize. You did in fact pose that aspect of the question. I didn't get to it.

The first thing almost that we did was to say that there's a lot of anecdotal evidence or anecdote about the performance of different building consent authorities. I think it's fair to say that the view you would have heard if you'd gone around the country and listened, as I did, was that some councils perform really well, others less so. And there are quite reasonable justifications for that.

Sometimes it might go to resources, it might go to the geography of an area, it might go to the challenges faced in the wake of large flooding events or other natural disasters that, of course, place a disproportionate impact in a particular geographical area at a particular time.

And of course, overall, we've got that scenario where the joint and several liability regime has meant that all councils have felt a great burden of responsibility to their ratepayers, and as a ratepayer- ... for it, that if something were to go wrong, if they were to miss some sort of defect or shortfall in terms of what was provided in the consent, the build as it was taking place as evidenced through inspections or the final sign-off, then all the ratepayers would be on the hook for up to 100% of the value of the cost of remediating.

So, changing to a system of proportionate liability we think will be helpful in that regard. And then to demonstrate the performance of those different councils, not for the purpose of singling out or as a witch hunt, but actually to say what's the reality on the ground, so I'm not relying on anecdotes and making recommendations.

We have collated and in fact required the provision of that data on consent processing times to MB. We've seen over the period of the two and a half years that we've been doing that an improvement, even as within that period of time, the number of applications has increased. So it's not just that there's less going on, so of course they can get through it faster. And credit to councils who have worked hard to make sure they do process in a very timely and efficient fashion.

If I were being cynical, I'd say summer is the best form of disinfectant. But in any case, all I can tell you is that we've seen improvement in that regard, such that 95.2% of consent applications were processed or completed within the statutory time frames in the year of our Lord, 2025. And as for inspections, some 98.3% of those were completed within three working days in the period of time since we introduced the inspection standards in 2025.

Of course, there's a technological ability through remote visual inspections to get these kinds of gains. And again, I applaud the councils that are getting on and doing exactly that. I'm conscious that we only have seven minutes of your time. I know you had a couple. Wait. Right. You wanted primary as well. I have, Julianne. Okay, sorry. Look, I'll go to Julianne.

But can we make both the questions and the answers a bit quicker, please? Because I've got some questions as well. Sure. I don't want to miss out mine. Julianne. In previous hearings, Minister, we've heard that building for climate change work was still happening. But I've had many discussions with people working in the sector who say it's basically stopped completely. And that not having that government support for more energy efficient buildings, both performance and construction, is slowing down the industry.

Do you have any comment to that? I certainly wouldn't expect that it would or should slow down. There's every incentive and increasingly an incentive for people to build in a sustainable way, whether that goes to embodied carbon or operational carbon in terms of the performance of the building, as you say. There's no reason that that shouldn't be the case.

Indeed, with other sources of energy other than solar panels, for example, and the technology of solar getting much improved in time and more affordable, and with regulatory changes we've made to enable that to be conducted without a building consent, and for faster processing of applications that include solar on rooftops.

That's an example of the fact that actually, if anything, the logic's getting increasingly Um, geared towards getting there sustainably. Banks that offer sustainability loans, I think they might call them green loans. You probably would enjoy that branding. But whatever, however they describe it. And of course, consumer choice. Increasingly, people are aware of the benefits. So there's no reason whatsoever that there should be less of that.

And I'd be surprised if there were. But do you think government has a role to play in lifting performance? Yeah, I think so. But I also think that the role of consumer choice, because our fellow Kiwis are so mindful of the benefits of greater insulation, for example, as such that we need to encourage and educate. But to mandate is not necessarily always the panacea. In fact, I would argue it's not in this case.

But certainly regulatory relief that we're able to provide, including faster consenting processing times for sustainable build plans. And it's not always straightforward to measure these things, but there are ways of doing it I think will make a great difference. So I'm really optimistic between that and the external factors that I've already outlined.

Is there going to be any shift to the metrics that were discussed in Building for Climate Change that would put us in a similar framework to places like British Columbia and so many other places overseas, where we're actually looking at kilowatt hours per meter squared as a metric of efficiency as opposed to the BPS?

Yeah. One of the changes that we ended up making was with the conversation around H1, which is, of course, the energy efficiency chapter within the building code. We've moved to methods of calculational modeling, which have a much more holistic way of ensuring that there is sustainability, but also human health and efficiency more generally in the design and build from a point of view of sustainability.

And that's, anecdotally at least, that's proven helpful. So there's no reason that we can't continue to improve our standards. But I don't have a comparative analysis with British Columbia or know the environmental factors such as their climate or the temperature range that might differ from ours. Well, I think it's just about the method of assessing what the minimum standard is.

Yeah. And whether that's done in a way that's looking at the overall- Yeah. I mean-... energy needed. So you're not just looking at mandating a certain amount of insulation. But- Yeah... can take into account window performance- Yeah... anything else too. Unless I've misunderstood your question, I think the answer is as I've provided, and apologies if I've genuinely misunderstood you, that the modeling method and even the calculation method are much better than the blunt instrument approach that we'd seen previously in terms of the way that was

put forward. That's certainly my understanding. But I would just say, for your benefit and for that of the Chair, if you want me to invite MBIE members or we can do- Do that. Yeah... is we could pick that up in the next session. Sure. Okay. Yep. Okay. Yep, can do that. Can I just ask, would you be willing to stay for another five minutes beyond the 4:00 if that's okay?

I think I'm available. That's okay. Please allow me to check. And if so, then of course I will happily stay. I normally have something at 4:00, but let's go another five for- Okay, great. Cool. That'd be lovely. Simon. Thank you, Mr. Chair. Minister Ping, I think this will be an easy one for you. Occupational licensing under this vote is intended to maintain practitioner competence, manage conduct and support confidence in the sector.

So given the recent controversy that we've heard around health regulators straying into ideological territory, are you confident that our tradies will only be regulated according to their performance and safety and not their political views? Yes, I am. I'm very confident of that. And these are conversations I'm happy to have with any approving authorities for occupational licensing.

Okay, so these are expectations that you'd be happy to set for these licensing authorities? Yes. Excellent. Thank you, Minister Ping. No, thank you. Two questions from me, which are both related. Two pieces of legislation that we had through our self-certifying plumbers and gasfitters. Yeah. And also the earthquake prone buildings. Both of them had subsequent work to be done.

We spent quite a bit of time on that, and we had those conversations. So really keen to understand where you've got to with the regulations sitting on plumbers and gasfitters. Yeah. Plumbers and drainlayers. Yeah. And also, we had a lot of discussion about the earthquake prone building methodologies, and that was actually pretty pivotal, we thought, in terms of not the one size fits all stuff and different model construction, different story levels and where the buildings are built into the ground and all that sort of stuff.

We got very exercised about, some in particular about Coastal Otago being included. Yeah. And it was how we deal with that. So really keen to get some steer on how that work's going. Yeah. Thank you. In terms of earthquake prone buildings, again, acknowledged and thank you for the work of this committee. In terms of Coastal Otago, the overarching point is that no one is going to be worse off in terms of their occupations.

Got that. Yes. It's the methodology that sits behind. We've got the legislation. Yeah. But it's the methodology. Yeah. I can't remember exactly what it was called. Help me, somebody who's in that. Matthew? You're talking about the national science model. Oh. Oh, you're talking with the zones. No, it's not the mo- It's- The design. Well, you might be talking about EPB methodology.

Yeah. That's it. The EPB methodology. So there's the EPB methodology sitting behind that, and then on the plumbers gasfitters one was the regulations. So those two, how they work makes a difference to, a real difference on the ground. Yeah. In terms of the plumbers and drainlayers, certainly the work of bringing forward the regulations to support and give effect to the primary legislation, and of course, it was the primary legislation that this committee considered, but also did some real important heavy lifting in terms of what the regs would look like to define relatively

straightforward work. And I think I'm right in saying those regulations have now been brought forward. But- The regulations were gazetted on the 9th of June. Outstanding. So the ink's drying on them as we speak. It's that hot off the press. Very good. Well done. If I was smarter, I would have sold that as an exclusive for the committee, but that relies on you not having an internet connection and knowing where to find the Gazette notices.

Yeah, the media are all over that one. And the earthquake prone. Yeah, I think the thrust of your question, and Matt will correct me if I'm wrong, perhaps in my absence, he said, looking at the clock. Yeah. Is that we want to be led by the science and where it's possible to say, for example, that Auckland, Northland, and Chatham Islands remain in a low seismic risk zone.

No, it was much more nuanced than that. It was really down to the actual individual building designs and the way it interfaced with ground- Okay... levels and all sorts of other stuff. I might leave you- Okay. We will canvass that one. It's just that really wanted to note that work is underway or being done and- Oh, okay... we'll deal with that. So perhaps if that was the question for you, for them, we could deal with it differently. It's certainly underway. Yeah. Okay, cool. Carlos, you had a final question?

Just very fast. Yesterday, you got another announcement to cut half of the working time on processing it. Yes. So probably if you still got time left, a little bit more information. But my question is more like, a lot of issues like you mentioned is on the government or local council level. How can you implement all those changes to ensure that council actually work with us or manage to adopt those changes?

Yeah. On the second one first, I will say that I've had really good experiences with councils, their leadership at elected and non-elected level, but also the individual building inspectors, including through the organization known as BOINZ, Building Officials Institute New Zealand. And they've given great feedback. They've told me about the issues and challenges they see.

So it's never been about saying we need to move faster or do things differently, therefore, we're going to impose that upon them. We can say, "Well, actually, what's the issue?" And sometimes when it comes to big picture items like liability settings, we've said simply, "Well, actually, you make a good point. Let's change things." And without obviously reducing the protections for the homeowners, which is why we've moved to the compulsory guarantee, warranty, and insurance system.

In terms of project information memoranda or memorandums, those who didn't do school C Latin, PIMs as you say, the point there is that where a granny flat is going to be built in an area that we don't want to exacerbate issues of flooding or natural hazards. We say you don't need a building consent or resource consent, but we do expect and hope that you will know what the nature of the property is. So a project information memorandum sets out the literal lie of the land. Councils need to provide it.

And if they take up to 20 working days, then you know better off than if you'd gotten a building consent in 20 working days. So to halve or in fact to specify a time within which that's got to be provided, namely 10 working days, is an exercise in ensuring some sort of consistency, certainty, and the gains that we are intending to see brought forward through the granny flat regime.

With that, really thank you for your, as always, open answers and for working together with us and with you over the last little while. Thank you. I'm glad you thought they were open. And thank you very much, team. I appreciate the opportunity. Cheers. Thank you. Thank you. Are there other officials who wish to come up to the table?

Some of the things we've been talking about earlier today. So a lot of work the team has been doing, and just wanted to acknowledge that, and also acknowledge that there are a lot of things that the team is doing that don't necessarily get the profile, because they don't involve primary legislation.

So for example, things like introducing a three-year building code review so the sector has certainty that changes will only happen on a three-year cycle, unless there's life safety issues, et cetera. There's a lot of work on data standards, which I think this committee might be interested in knowing is happening given your interest in the ongoing monitoring.

The various building code updates that are going on. And that's on top of the standard work of supporting the minister and looking after the building code, and all those sorts of things that we do. We do a lot of outreach as well. So yesterday we had a webinar with over 750 people taking part to talk about the various changes in the building system, and we do those on a reasonably regular basis as well.

I also did just want to talk about monitoring, because this is an issue that this committee raised last year, and I think it was an important issue to be raised. MBIE as an entity has quite strong data gathering capability, but we've really put a bit of an emphasis on it over the last year, and particularly in terms of translating that raw data into what actually may be able to be changed in the system.

And you've already asked questions around things like numbers of granny flats, overseas building products, the time frames for consenting, all those sorts of things. That's something that we're really trying to put a bit of an emphasis on, as well as the things that are coming up, the impacts of the self-certification for plumbing and drain laying, solar installations, and those sorts of things.

So I just really wanted to take the opportunity because it's something that was raised by this committee last year to just sort of raise that this is something that we are really trying to put a bit of a focus on, is monitoring the state of the system and how it's progressing insofar as we can. I think the data standards project that I referenced before is sort of trying to make sure that the data we're getting is actually fit for purpose in terms of actually giving a consistent story, and that's a bit of a challenge in its own right.

So those, sorry, Chair, I just wanted to make a few opening comments and- I think that's great. Thank you. It was always good to hear about some of the bits which are not quite so high profile, if you like. Yeah. Look, can I start off, and then I'm going to go Dan and Tangi. I'll go, yeah, and then Simon. And the only reason I'm going to start off is because I'd like a little bit more explanation of the earthquake-prone building methodology, and how that will play out. Because we got into that a little bit in the bill,

but clearly there was some deep mysteries involved in it, which will be good to have a little bit of explanation, because that makes a difference to people on the ground. Okay. And it'd be good to know what choices you've got within that methodology to say, "Well, we could do this or this," and- Yeah... has that implication. And sorry, you're probably all very familiar with Matt.

Oh, we are. We are. We're very pleased to have him. And probably most of my team, so they probably need no introduction. I want to choose Simon. I'm not sure if he's appeared. I think you've been here probably once or twice. Yeah. Simon Thomas, head of Building System Delivery and Assurance of— Yes. We've seen before. Yeah. Cool. Okay. That's the first area.

Thank you, Mr. Chair. The committee might recall, we did touch on it a little bit, but we didn't get a lot of time to dig into the detail on it. You might recall that the EPB methodology, first of all, it's an existing instrument in the current system. So we're migrating that across into the new regime going forward, but that framework already exists.

It will be a document that's made by chief executive, so it will be a rule under the earthquake-prone building legislation once it's passed. And the chief executive will have an obligation to go through a consultation process before that methodology is made. There'll be three parts to the methodology. There will be the high-level methodology, which will basically be a set of definitions and processes and procedures to go to identify an EPB and then to remediate an EPB.

It's quite a high-level document. Then there will be two annexes that go with the EPB methodology. The second of the two annexes will relate to the remediation options, which the committee had expressed some interest in a few weeks back. And then the first annex will outline the choices that are available to building owners in terms of going down the remediation pathway. The second of the two annexes will have that prescriptive detail, so there will be some standardized options there to remediate buildings so the building owners have got

clarity around what their remediation obligations are. And then there will be some more choice there around more complex remediation solutions, such as for heavy concrete buildings, where there will be some choices around identifying the high-risk deficiencies in the building. So rather than going through a full retrofit process, a building owner need only identify the most critically vulnerable parts of the building and remediate those parts of the building, rather than having to go to 34 or in excess of 34% of the NBS is required at the

moment. So generally speaking there, we'll be moving away from a one-size-fits-all kind of approach, which is the system that we have at the moment. Everyone must remediate to at least 34% of the NBS, to a much more flexible system. But we'll be focusing only on the most critical parts of the building, and the obligation will be to remediate those only.

So that's how we can start taking the cost out of the system. For the URM's, there was quite a lot of interest in unreinforced masonry buildings with the committee a few weeks back. The obligation there for one to two story URMs will be to secure the facades only. So that's the most attractive part of the building, and it's typically the part of the building that faces the street frontage or where pedestrian activity is.

And there will be some choices in there about how that can be done. So we are exploring some choices there around temporary remediation solutions. You might recall there was some discussion around creating more flexibility in the EPB methodology for temporary remediation solutions, right through to what we call facade mitigation, which will simply be to secure the facade to the building itself, which is generally it's just timber frames.

And the lessons that we learned from the Canterbury earthquakes is that, you might recall that a number of people lost their lives where the frontage of the building literally collapsed out onto the street, and people lost their lives because of the way in which the building failed in the earthquake. That will lead to something in the order of an 80% cost saving for those kinds of buildings.

And then for the other buildings, particularly for the heavy concrete buildings, if we can get building owners focused on the high-risk deficiencies then we will add down those in the EPB methodology and the annex system methodology. We can start to drive down the cost of those remediation solutions quite significantly as well.

So in short, all of that will be consulted on and things like- There's an obligation to go through a consultation process. Yeah. Yeah. Okay. So things like the widths of compost blocks, et cetera, which is the Oamaru stone stuff, and mezzanine floor things and floor heights and whatever. All of that will be subject to- All of that will be in the methodology and the annex system methodology. Yeah.

So watch that with a great deal of interest. I do recall there's quite a lot of interest in Oamaru stone from Oamaru. Yes. Yeah. And our engineer spoke to the committee on that and explained the very marginal differences there between a normal URM building and a Oamaru stone URM building. Not a lot in it, but.

I didn't think you said quite not a lot, but anyway, never mind. I was just being hopeful. Dan, and then Tangi. Just going back, before you mentioned about an amendment bill that's going to be coming before Parliament. Is that the Building Amendment Bill? Excellent Yeah, correct. So, some of the things that the minister was talking about are part of an amendment bill, which is, we're hoping, this is subject to Parliament's time, obviously, from an action- Liability settings Exactly. Liabilities, voluntary BCAs- Yeah

... all those things are part of the solar. Yeah. Will the bill also cover the mandatory home warranties, I think, is previously outlined last year? Is that going to be covering- That's the intention, yeah. Okay, great. We see that as a fairly fundamental part of changing the liability settings. Yeah.

Great. And any indication on where that bill is? Is it going to Cabinet soon? That's not something I can comment on. Yeah, no worries. For no other reason than it seems to change regularly . Yes. No worries. We look forward to seeing it. That's probably sound. Tanguy. Thank you, Mr. Chair. Thank you. The cuts to MBIE over the four years is just over 8.2 billion, sorry, million.

Come on, you're too used to NZTA. We've moved into building construction. Just over 8.2 million to the baseline. Now, there have been a number of developments and improvements, and my apologies if my colleague, Ms. Williams, asked this to the minister before, but she's obviously aware of a number of examples where previously there had been items that would have been imported.

The list here, for example, asbestos contaminated doors imported, increase in lead tap installations, blind cords, they do pose not just a risk, but a serious actual risk to young children. Failing imported windows that make entire wall systems leak. Given the extent of those cuts, what level of confidence do you have in the maintenance- I like your outfit... of quality and safety that that's actually not compromised as a result of the reduction in flow-through of finance?

So some of those things aren't necessarily building construction. But the ones that are, Simon Thomas' area is charged with doing those sorts of investigations. So perhaps he can speak to that. I think just before you do, I think while some of those items might not, I think from a consumer household perspective, there is concern about quality and safety across the board when it comes to someone in a property.

So yeah, just interested in your thoughts around that. Sure. Specifically with regard to building products, one of the functions within my team is to undertake investigations, both proactive and reactive, into building, specifically building product concerns. There are obviously other teams within MBIE who look at other consumer products.

Mm. You've raised the blinds issue in particular. The consumer protection team has looked at that in the past, and has issued some notices with regard to that, that I'm aware of. But our team, we undertake a number of work in the proactive space. We proactively review the building product information requirements for building products.

We actually have estimates measured for that next year of doing 5,000 of those reviews. We did about 7,500 this year, so confident in being able to continue to do that. Obviously, we also receive a number of complaints about various building products. You've mentioned windows. You've mentioned the asbestos and fire doors issue as well.

We will undertake those investigations as and when required. Dedicated team for those. We've had a couple of prosecutions recently that were successful. One in Auckland in relation to forgery with regard to some documents related to some fire protection systems, and one in Timaru for forgery in relation to a code mark certificate.

We have another couple of matters before the courts at the moment. So, we do look to undertake enforcement activity where we can. Yeah. Pleased to hear that. Just in terms of the team that undertakes that work- Yes... is there any reduction or change in headcount, in terms of FTEs doing that work?

Not in this coming year, no. Not in the coming year? But similar- No. We're maintaining our level of FTE, yes. Beyond that, I really can't speak to it at the moment. All right. Thank you. Simon. Right. Just coming back to standards and the system that you operate.

You mentioned that you update the building code every three years. What about the standards that sit behind it? So when the minister was in the chair, I referred to the concrete standard as material specifications, also a design standard. Is that something that's also updated every three years? Where is that on the program?

So we have a program of rolling reviews for all the standards, and in terms of the individual who spoke to you about a $50,000 cost- Mm... we would usually, if it's a requirement or included in an approved solution or a verification method, we would normally fund that completely through MBIE for those ones that we cite in our standards. So I'm not sure.

We'd need to find out a bit more about that specific- Again, that's anecdotal. Yeah. Yeah. Okay. Well, that's helpful. But how many standards are there for products, materials, design? I couldn't tell you off the top of my head, I'm afraid.
Andrew? If I may. Come forth.

I'll just grab this one. It's right under your seat from coming onto your seat. Sure, everyone. My name is Dave Gittings. I'm the manager of the Building Performance- Thanks... Engineering team that is responsible for the technical documents that supply for clients for building code and guidance. You talked about the standards. So we cite primary standards, around 300 standards within the compliance documents for building code.

Then there is secondary and tertiary, and they quickly come out to around the 3,000 mark. So- Wow... bringing them down to actually what the product standards are versus the design standards is quite difficult. Okay. How many standards do you think would sit with, say, consumer affairs in this matter, and how many sit in terms of within MB in this other role? Because I think when the minister mentioned that some of them might sit with another minister or another agency.

Yeah. And- We-- I'm sure we could get you that information. But remember, it's, Standards New Zealand have thousands of standards. Yeah. A relatively small proportion will relate to building construction. Okay. So it's hard for me to answer that question. Sorry.

It's all right. Thank you. Thanks, Mr. Chair. Other questions, colleagues? I've got one that I've- No. Okay. Do you want to go, Carlos? Yes. You know, this term of government, we actually changed a lot of policy to shorten the consenting time. So my question is more like, do long building consent timeframe result... Is it because of council inefficiency, unnecessary compliance requirement, or just they have a culture of delaying approval?

I think it's a combination of things. I think the minister talks about changing to the liability settings, moving away from joint several liability to proportional. Because joint and several liability necessarily brings a risk-averse approach, particularly if you're going to end up holding the can for a building that's gone wrong, and builders perhaps are phoenixed and are not available.

If you've had a fault, you get to pay for the whole lot. So there's a natural risk aversion in doing that. I think some of the processes that we've had have been a little bit unhelpful. And I think we're looking at speeding some of those things up, and the things we can do in terms of the regulator. I also think, so for example, small councils are not necessarily skilled to do sophisticated multi-story work in some cases, and they usually pass that on to the bigger metropolitan councils.

But at the moment, if they, for example, wanted to come together to form an entity to do that, they would effectively be precluded from doing it. So there are things in the regulations that we state that are actually not helping councils to be efficient, and that's what we're trying to address. But also, I actually got some feedback from the sector that sometimes it's the council culture causing the delay.

For example, they never will inform the developer all the problems or issues at one go. They want them to do one by one. They're causing a lot of back and forth. Yeah. Do you see that when you investigate those, or when you draft those new policy, do you see this as the problem, one of the problem? I think there are a range of issues, and I think some of the changes that we're making will help those.

Difficult for me to talk to that specifically, although I have heard that as well. Mm-hmm. The other issues I hear is concerns around things like fire reports, where a builder requires a fire report, and then the council gets its own fire report to check that the first one's okay. And then the two of them disagree with each other, and then things sort of grind to a halt.

Mm-hmm. Sometimes a third report's required. So there are things implicit within the system that are outside, in some ways, the control of the council, that are leading to inefficiencies. Yeah, so we are aware of that, and we're hoping that some of the reforms that we're bringing in will help to address some of those things.

Cool. Thank you. Tania. Thank you. The use of AI, what do you understand is the impact on the work that you do within MB as a result of AI effectively sort of not just coming on spec, but the increasing opportunities and, I guess, some limitations or concerns that might exist with that as well?

Yeah, I think it's a huge opportunity to look at our systems and make better use of the technology to make things more efficient. In fact, Dr. Gittings here is sort of helping. So we've got a multi-stream approach. One of them is to look at where we can do things internally more efficiently.

So, the minister talked about building product information requirements and how that is quite a time-intensive process. We've done a pilot with AI to do that, reduced the time-intensive manual process by a matter of weeks, so just by using AI to do checks on building product information requirements.

So there's a lot of things we can do internally. And I think, to be fair, we're at the start of that process. But we also know there's a lot of things in the sector that could be changed and will be changed through AI. And so there are at least two private entities now that have a AI system to check building consents.

So it's not proposed or promoted as a replacement of the building inspection process, but it is something that architects and designers can use to run a building through and give them some red flags where things may not be up to building code before it hits the council.

So there's a lot of good innovation, both internally to MB and externally, around the use of AI. It's obviously an emerging area. It's an area we need to be a bit careful of. But certainly, there are significant prospects for efficiencies The efficiencies that you touch on relate to this sort of systemic approach to how business might be done.

Are there any indications as to an impact on head count as a result of taking up some of these emerging AI opportunities? Nothing. So we've piloted some work, and the impacts of that may have impacts on head count. But at this point in time, it would be premature for me to speculate on what that might look like, if at all.

Can I pursue a little bit more? So just with this, $8.2 million over a four-year period, where do you see those savings being made on the shop floor? So when you talk about 8.2, are you talking about moving forward? Yeah. So there's, I think, $2.2 to $2.7 million reduction in the baseline in the first year, $2 million subsequent years after that. It's one thing to say, well, this budget delivers baseline savings of $8.2 million.

What I want to know from you is what you translate that to mean in terms of the operations that you do. Yeah. The impact that that might have on staff. Or your capacity to provide policy advice or-... to respond to those sorts of requests. Yeah. So where do you see those savings? So in the current budget for next year, there's about a $4.5 million reduction.

And that reduction has come via a winding down of Weathertight Services. It's come because we had a project to realign New Zealand and Australian and international standards. Mm-hmm. That's something that's ongoing, but we were doing a catch-up, so that was 1.5 million that has been removed from the budget.

We are first of all committed to the obligations under the Climate Change Response Act, which is a trend towards net zero by 2050. And there are provisions in the emissions reduction plan and the national adaptation plan where we have a program of work underway as a contribution to that.

But that contribution is now taking more of a market-based approach where we let the market lead, take a greater role for the market. But there are some key things that MBIE is still doing there that will help the market make better informed decisions for themselves about how it's going to adapt to climate change over time. So for example, we have a national carbon repository where that can be accessible. It's on the pilot phase at the moment, which will allow building owners and architects and so on to determine the overall embodied carbon in the building

using different building techniques. So that's one thing. So there's an incentive there by using that methodology to provide the operational emissions and the embodied emissions in their building designs. That's one thing. And then there is a pilot underway to look at a register where you may be able to look into the embodied carbon products as well.

So building owners and architects and so on can access these databases to determine what might be the overall emissions in their building designs. And that's taken a much more market-based approach. And that's all about embodied, yeah. Yes, it is embodied, yeah. Anything on operational? Dave, do you want to take that?

Yeah. So we are continuing with some- Yes... the technical work and that kilowatts per square meter that you spoke about. If I may add a couple of other points of clarification to your question earlier on. I think you had a question on energy efficiency retrofits, as I recall. And that is one of the actions in ERP2, is to explore ways in which we can make it easier for householders to retrofit existing dwellings. So that's an action that we, we're committed to and we'll be looking at in terms of future program of work. So it may give some opportunities there for us to explore that's down the track.

And the other thing that we've been working on in partnership with EECA and New Zealand Green Building Council is exploring the wider rollout of the neighbors, which you're familiar with. Mm-hmm.
